stories.headline: Avalanche Season Ends in Stunning Sweep as Golden Knights Win Game 4, 2-1
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche's season came to an abrupt end Tuesday night in Las Vegas, as the Golden Knights completed a four-game sweep with a 2-1 victory in Game 4 of the Western Conference Finals at T-Mobile Arena. The loss is a stunning reversal for a team that entered the series with an 8-1 playoff record — a franchise record for fewest games needed to reach a conference final.

Goaltender Mackenzie Blackwood, starting in place of Scott Wedgewood, was the Avalanche's best player, making a series of brilliant saves to keep the game close. However, the team in front of him managed just one shot on goal in the first 12 minutes of the third period, and Vegas sealed the win on a deflection with just over five minutes remaining. Gabriel Landeskog scored a late goal with Blackwood pulled, but it was not enough.

The Golden Knights dominated in scoring chances throughout, outshooting Colorado 27-11 through two periods. Nathan MacKinnon, who had been listed as questionable after leaving Game 3 with an injury, played but could not generate the offense needed to extend Colorado's season.

"It's empty; there's no other way to describe it, really," said Gabriel Landeskog. Vegas will now advance to its third Stanley Cup Final in the franchise's nine-year history.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis Signs Bills Overhauling RTD Board, Reshaping Front Range Passenger Rail District
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis on Tuesday signed two significant transportation bills that will reshape how mass transit is governed along Colorado's Front Range. Senate Bill 150 reduces the RTD board from 15 members to nine, with five elected from separate districts starting in 2028 and four appointed by the governor and confirmed by the state Senate. Appointed members must collectively hold expertise in finance, land use, transportation planning, and transit equity, and at least one must be a current or former union member.

Senate Bill 172 narrows the Front Range Passenger Rail District's boundaries to include only municipalities directly along Interstate 25 that will be served by the planned rail line, stretching from Trinidad in the south to Fort Collins in the north, including Denver and several metro communities. Starter rail service, funded at $332 million, is expected to begin by January 2029.

Polis also signed House Bill 1313, which loosens the requirements local governments must meet to qualify for affordable housing funding under Proposition 123. Sponsors said rising construction costs had made existing requirements nearly impossible to meet even for proactive communities.

"Coloradans deserve affordable, reliable, convenient transportation to get where we want to go, and that starts with more efficiency, accountability and transparency," Polis said in a statement accompanying the RTD bill signing.

stories.headline: Update: Truck Driver Cited After Falling Asleep Caused Fatal I-70 Crash in Wheat Ridge
stories.summary:
Wheat Ridge police announced Tuesday that the driver of a truck involved in a deadly weekend crash on Interstate 70 fell asleep at the wheel before veering off the road and flipping perpendicular across the highway, causing four oncoming vehicles to collide. Rayshawn Cox, 32, was cited on suspicion of careless driving resulting in death and two counts of careless driving causing serious bodily injury.

The crash occurred at approximately 2:40 a.m. Saturday on eastbound I-70 just west of Kipling Street. An 18-year-old man was killed, and another driver and a passenger in Cox's truck were seriously injured. Cox and his passenger have since been released from the hospital. The Jefferson County Coroner's Office said it would not release the victim's name at the family's request.

The truck was not heavy enough to require a commercial driver's license, police said. Investigators noted the case remains open and that additional charges could be filed as more evidence emerges. Cox's next court date had not been scheduled as of Tuesday.

The crash prompted a 12-hour closure of eastbound I-70 in west Denver over the weekend.

stories.headline: Denver Smoke Shop Suspended After Repeatedly Selling Tobacco, Kratom to Minors
stories.summary:
Denver officials suspended the tobacco license of Vibe Smoke Shop, 7530 E. Colfax Ave., on Tuesday after workers repeatedly sold tobacco and kratom to minors and were found to be illegally selling alcohol, psilocybin, and marijuana, according to city records. The Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection called it "one of the worst cases of alleged illegal product sales the city has ever uncovered in random inspections."

The violations date back to June 2025, when undercover teenage operatives successfully purchased tobacco products from the store on multiple occasions. Community complaints escalated in January after two teenage boys were found purchasing kratom products containing 7-hydroxymitraginine, a highly potent, opioid-like compound. City officials said both boys developed symptoms of kratom addiction and required outside addiction treatment services.

An unannounced inspection in late March found large quantities of alcohol, flavored tobacco products, "magic mushroom" tablets, high-THC marijuana products, and elevated-7-OH kratom products on the premises. The store holds neither a liquor nor a marijuana license. Flavored tobacco products are banned in Denver, and 7-OH levels in kratom are regulated under state law.

Vibe Smoke Shop must appear for a virtual hearing on June 26 to argue why its license should not be permanently suspended or revoked. Phone calls to the store went unanswered Tuesday.

stories.headline: Founding Documents Arrive at Denver International Airport as Part of U.S. 250th Anniversary Tour
stories.summary:
The "Freedom Plane," operated by the National Archives, landed at Denver International Airport Tuesday morning carrying original founding documents of the United States as part of a nationwide tour marking the country's 250th anniversary. The arrival was accompanied by a public celebration and press conference at the airport.

Following the ceremony, the documents were transferred to the History Colorado Center, where they will be on display for the public from May 28 through June 14. Denver is one of eight cities on the tour.

The National Archives tour is designed to bring some of the nation's most historically significant documents directly to Americans across the country in the lead-up to the semiquincentennial celebration.

stories.headline: Denver Fusion Energy Company Xcimer Expands Operations, Plans to Double Workforce
stories.summary:
Xcimer Energy, a Denver-based company developing laser-driven fusion energy technology, is expanding its local footprint with 16,000 square feet of additional office space at 4880 Havana St., effective June 1. The company, which already operates a 74,000-square-foot headquarters and laser facility on the northeast side of the city, plans to grow its workforce from approximately 150 employees to more than 300 over the next year.

Founded in Silicon Valley in 2022, Xcimer relocated to Colorado in 2024. The company has built what it calls the largest privately owned laser system in the world, with a goal of constructing a pilot fusion power plant by 2035. Xcimer's approach uses a gas-based laser system that is an order of magnitude cheaper than conventional fusion laser technology, according to co-founder and CTO Alexander Valys.

Fusion energy, which powers stars by fusing light atomic nuclei under extreme heat, produces low-level, short-lived radioactive waste — distinct from the long-lasting waste generated by fission-based nuclear plants. Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory achieved fusion ignition for the first time in 2022, a milestone Xcimer is building upon with its commercial-scale approach.

"Fusion is the last and best energy source that humanity is ever going to develop. There's nothing on the horizon after fusion," Valys said in an interview with The Denver Post.

stories.headline: Free Westracks Shuttle Launches, Connecting Denver-Area Light Rail to Red Rocks and Other Western Destinations
stories.summary:
A long-planned free shuttle service connecting Denver metro light rail to popular outdoor destinations west of the city launched over Memorial Day weekend. The Westracks shuttle runs Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays through Labor Day, departing every 15 minutes from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. from the RTD station near the Jefferson County Government Center in Golden.

Stops include Red Rocks Park, Matthews/Winters Park, Dinosaur Ridge, downtown Morrison, and the Morrison Natural History Museum. Riders can either park at the Golden RTD station or connect directly from the light rail line. The service is aimed at reducing traffic and parking congestion while expanding access to residents who lack vehicles.

"Access to our region's most iconic landscapes shouldn't depend on owning a vehicle or winning a battle for parking," said Jefferson County Parks and Open Space Director Aaron Roth. Advocates noted that many Denver residents, particularly from lower-income communities, have never visited destinations like Red Rocks due to transportation barriers.

The three-year pilot program costs approximately $1 million, with the majority funded through an RTD grant. Jefferson County is among several local governments and agencies partnering on the initiative. The shuttle currently serves daytime visitors only and does not operate for evening concerts at Red Rocks.

stories.headline: Rockies' Pitching Woes and Power Shortage Put Team on Pace for Fourth Straight 100-Loss Season
stories.summary:
Through 55 games, the Colorado Rockies own the lowest winning percentage in Major League Baseball at .364 (20-35), with the worst run-differential in the majors at minus-65. At their current pace, the Rockies would finish 59-103, which would make them the first team since the 1961-64 Washington Senators to suffer four consecutive 100-loss seasons.

The starting rotation has been the primary culprit in May. Colorado starters posted a 7.11 ERA in the month with a 2-13 record, including an 18-start stretch without a quality start — the third-longest drought in franchise history. Of the three veteran starters signed this offseason for a combined $19.1 million, Michael Lorenzen has been particularly poor with a 7.21 ERA, while Jose Quintana is now on the 15-day injured list after exiting Sunday's start with elbow pain and Chase Dollander remains weeks away from throwing again.

Tomoyuki Sugano has been solid with a 3.86 ERA, and right-hander Ryan Feltner is expected to return to the rotation this week after missing time with right ulnar nerve inflammation. A lack of power has compounded pitching struggles, with just a handful of hitters posting above-average offensive numbers.

Manager Warren Schaeffer and the organization have pointed to an 11-game improvement over last season's historically bad start as a measuring stick for progress, but the data heading into June suggests the Rockies remain far from competitiveness.

stories.headline: Denver Weather: Warm Tuesday Marks Last Dry Day Before Week of Rainfall and Fire Danger
stories.summary:
Tuesday is expected to be Denver's last dry day of the week, with a forecast high of around 81 degrees accompanied by gusty winds reaching up to 40 mph, according to the National Weather Service. The combination of heat, dry conditions, and strong winds has prompted elevated fire danger warnings across much of Colorado, and residents are urged to remain fire-conscious throughout the day.

A sustained period of rainfall is expected to move into the Denver area beginning Wednesday, with the largest precipitation totals forecast for that day. Scattered storms are expected to continue through much of the remainder of the week, offering some relief from dry conditions that have contributed to drought concerns along the Front Range.

The shift in weather patterns is notable given ongoing concerns about bear activity, wildfire risk, and beetle infestations tied to the region's prolonged dry spell.

stories.headline: Denver Distiller Launches 'Curated Barrel Project,' Aims to Shift Whiskey Culture Away from Collecting
stories.summary:
Jake Norris, a longtime Denver resident and veteran distiller behind some of Colorado's most sought-after specialty whiskey releases, has launched The Curated Barrel Project, a new venture designed to push back against the collector mentality that has come to define American whiskey culture. Norris spent more than a decade at Stranahan's and Laws Whiskey House and helped popularize limited-release programs that inadvertently fueled secondary-market speculation.

The project will release eight different whiskey expressions over the next couple of years, each using the same base bourbon recipe aged in different cask types to produce distinct flavor profiles. The first release, called The Control, debuted this month — a seven-year, unfinished bourbon with a mash bill of 75% corn, 21% rye, and 4% malted barley, with approximately 400 bottles available. Future releases will be finished in Cognac, Port wine, and other spirit barrels.

To discourage speculation and encourage consumption, Norris is pairing each release with chef-driven pop-up dinners and including a companion zine with each bottle. The whiskey originated from 30 barrels Norris contracted in 2019 that sat through the pandemic, ultimately aging into a well-developed spirit he decided to release himself.

In addition to the project, Norris is working to open a new distillery in Arvada, further cementing his presence in the Colorado spirits industry.

stories.headline: Update: Castle Rock Man Who Died on Manitou Incline Identified as Nickolay Jogolev, Father of Seven
stories.summary:
The man who died on the Manitou Incline during a Cub Scout outing over the weekend has been identified as Nickolay Jogolev, 48, of the Castle Rock area. Jogolev was hiking with his 9-year-old son and other Cub Scouts on May 23 when he experienced a medical emergency around the 900th step of the steep trail near Manitou Springs, west of Colorado Springs.

A passerby offered Jogolev a Gatorade after noticing he appeared pale, according to a 9News report citing his wife. He drank it, stood up, and collapsed shortly thereafter. Emergency responders closed the trail for approximately two hours while Jogolev was transported to a hospital, where life-saving efforts were unsuccessful. He had no known health conditions and had previously trained for the hike at the Parker Incline.

Jogolev leaves behind his wife, Coral, and seven children in their blended family, some of whom have special needs. A GoFundMe campaign set up to support the family had already raised more than $35,000 as of Tuesday.

The Manitou Incline covers 2,768 steps with a vertical gain of 2,000 feet in less than a mile and is considered one of the most strenuous hikes in the country.

stories.headline: Rockies' Mickey Moniak Emerges as Surprise All-Star Candidate in Career Comeback Season
stories.summary:
Mickey Moniak, the first overall pick in the 2016 MLB Draft who went through years of struggles and a release before landing in Colorado, has quietly become one of the Rockies' best players and a legitimate All-Star candidate heading into the summer. Through his games this season, Moniak leads the club with 12 home runs, 28 RBIs, and 91 total bases, along with an OPS+ of 149 — well above the MLB average of 100 and far ahead of any other Rockies hitter.

Moniak was picked up by Colorado as a free agent in 2025 after the Los Angeles Angels released him just before Opening Day, and he is now in his second season with the organization. His All-Star case is enhanced by a compelling narrative: the Midsummer Classic this year is scheduled for July 14 in Philadelphia, the city where Moniak was originally drafted by the Phillies.

The 28-year-old is currently on the 10-day injured list after suffering an ankle injury May 13 in Pittsburgh and batting just 2-for-23 in the games that followed. His return timeline will be critical to whether he maintains his statistical lead and earns a spot on the National League roster.

Despite the Rockies' overall struggles — the team entered Tuesday with the worst winning percentage in baseball — Moniak has provided one of the few bright storylines in Denver this season.

joke.joke: I have a hard time ordering at restaurants. I see what other people are eating and I just want that. I don't even need a menu.

topStory.headline: Skydiver Killed, Second Injured After Midair Parachute Collision Near Ritzville, Washington
topStory.summary:
A skydiver was killed and a second was injured Sunday evening after their parachutes collided approximately 1,000 feet above the ground during a group jump near Ritzville, Washington, about 180 miles east of Seattle. The Adams County Sheriff's Office and Skydive West Plains identified the deceased as Randy Hubbs, 70, of the Kennewick area, a real-estate broker and longtime member of the drop zone community. The injured jumper, Nicole Klein of the Colville area, suffered a hip injury and was transported to a local hospital, where she is expected to make a full recovery.

Hubbs and Klein were among 11 skydivers participating in a group free-fall jump at Skydive West Plains when their canopies made contact, according to a statement from the company. Neither jumper was aware of the other's position at the time of the collision. The impact left Hubbs incapacitated and no longer in control of his parachute; staff on the ground observed him at roughly 500 feet, unresponsive with his head and arms down, drifting north away from the designated drop zone before he was found dead at the scene.

Both skydivers were described as experienced. Hubbs had completed more than 800 jumps and Klein more than 900 prior to the incident, according to Skydive West Plains. Authorities said weather was not a contributing factor in the collision, and both jumpers had deployed their parachutes successfully before the canopies came into contact.

Skydive West Plains issued a statement mourning Hubbs, saying he had been part of the drop zone community for nearly a decade. Friends paid tribute on social media, with one acquaintance writing that a skydiving exit "feels exactly like Randy." The Adams County Sheriff's Office said the investigation into the incident remains ongoing.
